description: Registry of every web game built by gh-game-factory. Never duplicate an entry — check name, description, category, and genre before building.

games.name: deepwatch
games.description: Silent co-op card game of nerve for 2-4 divers: surface a hand of deep-sea creatures onto one shared line from shallowest to deepest, where NO number appears anywhere (a card's height on the depth gauge IS its depth) and there is no chat channel by design — the only information is who hasn't played yet and how long they've been not-playing. A misplay dredges up every shallower card and burns one of a few SHARED air tanks. The signature twist is the sonar ping, spendable only when every diver holds their card at once. One card, two gestures (tap to surface, press-and-hold to signal) — and a hold can never resolve as a tap, or every signal would surface a card. Live P2P co-op; hands are NEVER transmitted, only derived from (seed, level) minus what has left play, which makes host transfer a no-op state-wise (proven by tests/takeover.test.ts). Three modes: Shallows, Trench, Abyss (crowded deck + you only ever see your next card). A mandatory co-op balance sim OVERRULED five confident diagnoses: the rising tide never fired at all; sonar was WORSE than not having it (7% vs 8%) until divers learned to JOIN each other's signals (+5.35 sigma over 6,300 paired runs); widening the deck did nothing (proportional-only jitter makes every absolute scale cancel); scaling air tanks didn't rescue big crews even at +3/diver; and a broken control arm hid it all (sonar:false zeroed pings once, but modes grant bonus charges at pingAt levels). The real fixes were structural: a tide budget PER CARD, and a dive whose depth scales DOWN as the crew grows so every party faces the same total cards. Crucially the sim also certified a level 1 that ran in 6.3s — a real human got ZERO taps in before the tide surfaced their cards, because the sim's diver judges instantly; the tide was slowed to human speed and JITTER raised until the curve returned. Also found and mutation-guarded a lingering countdown-overlay leak on the live site.
games.status_note: Verified in-browser on the LIVE production URL at true 375x812 for EVERY mode (Shallows/4 -> 5 levels, Trench/4 -> 7, Abyss/4 -> 8): zero horizontal overflow, card fully on-screen and >=44px, four crew chips with no overlap, footer hidden mid-round, no stray [hidden] layer, zero console errors. Two-peer P2P smoke test NOT run against a second live browser context; host transfer is covered by 13 automated takeover tests including 'can still reach game over'.

games.name: emberwake
games.description: Neon lane-runner built on one legible rule: momentum is your speed, your health AND your score at once. A wall of dark chases at a floor speed that rises with distance, so clipping a hazard never kills you outright — it bleeds momentum, you slow, and the dark closes. The design consequence is the whole game: sidestepping into an empty lane is always safe and pays nothing, while THREADING a hazard in-lane (jump the pit you're standing in, slide under the bar) pays the refill you need to afford the next chasm. Chasms are crossable only while SURGING, and surging spends the very momentum keeping you ahead. Swipe controls (lane/jump/slide) + a held SURGE pad — no D-pad. Solo score-attack; async-seed multiplayer via a worldwide UTC daily seed, share links carrying seed+mode+distance, and a translucent ghost of your own best run on that track — all byte-exact because the sim is a pure function of (seed, mode, inputs). Results show a deterministic bot's 'clean line' distance on your exact seed so you know what you left on the table. Three modes with real spread: Ember (3 lanes, balanced), Nightfall (3 lanes, dark starts close and gains fast, mote-sparse), Latticework (5 lanes, denser hazards, gentler dark, longest runs). The mandatory balance sim OVERRULED the design twice — with no momentum decay the bot pinned momentum at max and cruised 6-9 minutes to a fixed wall with a 3% spread and zero skill differentiation; over-correcting with heavy decay ended every run by starvation BEFORE the first chasm, making the signature surge mechanic irrelevant in 2 of 3 modes and scoring all policies identically. Shipped economy is the third attempt: mixed surge beats pure-hoarding 3.3-4.3x and surge-spam 4.8-7.3x, <=3% chasm fumbles, zero unfair early deaths. Fairness is structural (strictly-increasing event distances => a safe lane always exists, pinned by a test).
games.status_note: Deploy succeeded and production serves correctly over HTTP: every asset 200 (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest, all four icons, og, robots, sitemap), and the shipped JS+CSS are a byte-for-byte SHA256 match for the exact local build that was fully play-verified in-browser at 375px across all three modes. NOT marked deployed because GitHub's TLS cert for the custom domain was still stuck in state 'new' after ~70min (early CNAME cycling likely requeued it), and both browser surfaces refuse plain HTTP, so a visual pass against the LIVE https URL could not be taken. Flip to deployed once https://emberwake.benrichardson.dev serves 200 and a ~375px per-mode screenshot is taken.

games.name: scrapwall
games.description: Co-op grid base-defence: wall a central Core from shared scrap, feed auto-turrets the ammo you harvest from renewable salvage nodes, and hold an ever-worsening horde that pathfinds to the Core along the path of LEAST RESISTANCE — a weighted-Dijkstra flow-field routes husks around strong walls and digs through your weakest one (a real routing bug was found here — the flow-field weighted the source cell instead of the entered cell, luring husks INTO walls — and mutation-guarded). Over-fortifying starves the guns of the shared ammo pool, so the fort is a budget not a wish-list. Tap-to-build, drag-to-paint a wall run, tap-to-harvest — a board-game control scheme, no D-pad. Solo-first; live P2P co-op for 2-4 (host-authoritative snapshot star, actions on 'act', full grid+horde snapshot at 10Hz); host transfer promotes a survivor who rebuilds the deterministic spawn queue and keeps the run finishable. Three modes that change the SPACE not a dial: Outpost 9x9/two edges, Depot 11x11/four edges, Sprawl 13x13/four edges. Co-op scaling is honest — the horde and the scrap income both scale with party count (peer leaving shrinks the horde), refereed by a mandatory balance sim (AI-vs-the-horde over hundreds of seeds): a smooth wave ramp with a contested middle, every run terminates (the immortal-turret-carpet trap was caught + fixed), party playable at every size, RAMP_EXP pinned as the load-bearing termination constant. Full two-peer smoke test passed on live production: typed-code join, in-sync co-op play, host-leave -> survivor keeps playing, rematch into a fresh shared round.

games.name: tiny-tanks
games.description: Top-down two-team capture-the-flag tank duel: your shots RICOCHET off the walls (bank them around corners; your own rebound can kill you), steal the enemy flag and race it home, first team to 3 wins. Two-hit tanks + a grab-invuln make a flag run a real, winnable race; nobody is eliminated (respawn); power-ups drop mid-field (spread, rapid, speed, shield, extra ricochet). Always a 2v2 with bots backfilling empty seats, so solo (you + a bot ally vs two bots) and 2-player both feel full. Live P2P versus (2-4 humans), host-authoritative snapshot star, host transfer promotes a survivor, rematch inside the room with a running match tally. Twin floating analog sticks on touch (left drives, right aims + auto-fires); WASD + mouse on desktop. PORTRAIT arena (bases top/bottom) so it fills a phone. Three modes that change the SPATIAL problem (Clash open, Labyrinth dense cover, Rampart structured chokes). The sim is PROVABLY point-symmetric (a mirror-input fairness test keeps seats exactly mirrored) so team fairness is by construction; the mandatory balance sim (48-55% team rate, P(lead@1) 66-72%) OVERRULED the design repeatedly — one-shot kills gave ZERO bot captures, fixed with two-hit tanks + grab-invuln + return-path cover, and it caught a self-symmetric centre pickup the lower seat always won (removed).

games.name: grainfall
games.description: Hammurabi-style micro-civilisation economy sim: rule a city-state one year at a time, splitting your grain three ways — trade land at a price that swings each year, sow the fields (a bushel of seed per acre, a farmer works 10), and feed your people (20 bushels each) — then the year rolls: harvest (weather sets bushels/acre), rats, plague, and settlers. Lose >45% of your people to starvation in a year and you're deposed. Solo-first with async seed-share (Daily Reign + challenge links replay a byte-identical deterministic decade; results screen shows a masterful-steward benchmark on your exact seed). Three genuinely different reigns: Steward (10y balanced), Famine (11y defensive attrition, break-even economy), Dynasty (18y boom + land speculation). The economy IS the game: a balance sim (cautious vs greedy bots, 400 seeds/mode) OVERRULED the design twice — Famine was mathematically unwinnable (net food per farmed person < feed need) and Dynasty's blind auto-growth swelled the city past its land's feeding capacity; fixed with break-even tuning + a settler land-cap (mutation-verified). A ~375px mobile HUD number-clipping bug was caught in the per-mode browser pass and pinned with a CSS-invariant guard.

games.name: gloamrun
games.description: Endless co-op dungeon crawler: descend procedurally generated floors against an ever-worsening swarm — you auto-fire at the nearest monster so the game is all in your feet (position, kite, dash with i-frames), clear the floor, step on the stair, draft one of three upgrades, go deeper. Downed players are revivable by a teammate, never eliminated; the run ends only on a party wipe, and clearing a floor revives the party (floors are checkpoints). Fully playable solo; live P2P co-op for 2-4 over a shared room code. Host-authoritative star (host runs monster sim + floor machine and broadcasts a full snapshot, clients send input and predict their own hero); host transfer promotes a survivor who keeps the run finishable. Three modes with real spread (Delve roomy, Onslaught crowds, Gauntlet tight elites), boss every few floors. The difficulty curve is the opponent: a mandatory sim (tests/balance.test.ts) refereed the ramp and caught a 4-player immortal-party bug (out-DPSing a polynomial ramp forever) — fixed with a geometric HP term past floor 5 that guarantees termination. Also back-ported the stale rematch `cur` catch-up fix into patterns/.

games.name: delvepack
games.description: Co-op top-down dungeon crawler: descend procedurally-generated floors, auto-fire at the nearest monster, dash to dodge (i-frames), clear a floor to drop upgrade orbs and light the exit rune, then stand on it to descend — the ramp is the whole difficulty. Solo score-attack (deepest floor) plus live P2P for 2-4 delvers in the SAME deterministic dungeon against the dungeon, reviving downed teammates over a bleed-out window (all-down = wipe; a dropped peer just makes the run harder). Host-authoritative snapshot star: host owns monsters/bolts/HP/floor/rune, each peer owns only its own delver's motion; host transfer promotes a survivor who rebuilds combat stats from the deterministic formula and keeps the run finishable. Three modes that change the SPACE not a dial (Delve mid-hall, Warren wide crowd-cavern, Crypt tight knife-fight). NOTE: built concurrently with (and independent of) ben-gy/gloamrun from the same IDEAS.md line — a shared-queue race; both shipped. The co-op difficulty curve is refereed by an AI-vs-the-dungeon balance sim that OVERRULED the design (crowds+revive-cascades made co-op a tax, not deeper) and surfaced two real softlocks (a monster unkillable behind a pillar; an orb-fixation stall), both fixed and mutation-guarded.

games.name: morsel
games.description: Petri-dish free-for-all: eat pellets to grow, swallow any smaller blob whole, run from bigger ones. Big blobs are slow, so the only way to catch anyone is to DASH — and a dash costs 15% of your mass, sprayed out behind you as food for whoever you're chasing, so the only way to grow your lead is to spend it. Pellets ramp in value over the round (the bloom) so an early lead banks nothing and the last stretch decides it; being swallowed just costs 1.5s and nobody is ever out. Three modes (Skirmish tiny/6/110s, Petri medium/6/150s, Famine big/8/180s with a third of the food); solo vs bots, live P2P free-for-all for 2-8, or an async seed-share. Split authority: each peer owns its own blob's motion and death, the host owns the pellet field, bots, clock and mass ledger; host transfer promotes a survivor who keeps the round finishable. Balance simulated AI-vs-AI (P(leader wins) flat early then spikes, every seat within a few points of chance) — the design doc predicted a snowball; the sim proved the opposite and drove the fix.

games.name: driftlock
games.description: Abstract board game on a wrapping grid where you never touch a stone — you shift the whole row or column it stands on, everything slides and wraps, and any stone that lands in the central Well banks for whoever made the shift (stones are neutral, so there is no seat geometry to make unfair). The line you just shifted is locked for your opponent's next turn, so the game is about leaving them nothing to take. The Well rises as a pure function of the move number and stones are worth more the later they land, so an early lead is not a won game — a design the balance sim refereed and overruled repeatedly (documented in src/game.ts). Solo vs 3-strength bots, live P2P for 2, and async seed-share; three modes (Blitz 5-stone, Duel 9-stone, Deepwell 9x9).

games.name: windup
games.description: Simultaneous-programming strategy: slot three movement cards into your wind-up bot, then every bot's program executes at once, one step at a time — and bots shove each other, so where you end up is rarely where you planned. Cogs ripen (+1 value a round, up to 5) so the board starts cheap and ends enormous, and everyone converges on the fat centre cog at the climax. Falling in a pit costs tempo, never elimination. Three modes (Skirmish 9x9/8 rounds, Clockwork 11x11/12, Gauntlet 13x13/16 with four program slots); solo vs bots, live P2P for 2-4, or an async seed-share challenge. Board is 90-degree rotationally symmetric with cogs spawning in symmetric orbits, so turn-0 is provably fair.
games.status_note: Deploy succeeded and every production asset serves 200 (HTML, JS, CSS, manifest, all four icons), with the [hidden] guard confirmed in the shipped CSS. Full in-browser play + two-peer P2P smoke test (typed-code join, in-sync play, host stickiness, host-leave -> survivor finishes) all passed against the byte-identical local build of this commit. NOT marked deployed because the browser tooling (Chrome extension + in-app pane) went down before a visual pass against the live URL and a ~375px mobile screenshot could be taken.

games.name: bidstorm
games.description: Bidding card game with no luck in the deal: every player holds the identical hand 1-13 and secretly commits one card per flipped prize, highest bid takes the pot and every card played is burned forever, while a tie for the lead wins nothing and carries the pot into the next round. Every hand is public, so it's a decision rather than a guess. Three modes (Classic, Foresight's two-prize lookahead, Blitz's seven cards where a tie burns the prize); solo vs bots, live P2P for 2-6, or an async seed-share challenge.

games.name: lumenlock
games.description: Light-routing puzzle: a white beam hits a prism and fans into a rainbow (red bends left, green straight, blue right); flip mirrors and half-silvered splitters to lock every crystal in its exact colour, where a crystal sums everything it receives so a stray beam spoils it and a white crystal takes either untouched white light or red+green+blue recombined. Solo, with an exhaustively-searched true par, a UTC Daily Lock and seeded share links.

games.name: nightwire
games.description: Hidden-role social deduction on a ring of seats: each night you probe a seat and learn how many Ghosts sit in that 3-seat window, but while Crew readings publish automatically and truthfully, Ghosts choose what number to publish — eject every Ghost before the nightly wire cuts cause a blackout; solo vs solver bots or live P2P for 4-10 humans.

games.name: orbital-skirmish
games.description: Momentum dogfight in a circular arena with a lethal star at the centre that pulls ships, bullets AND rocks — so you never aim where a rival is, you aim where gravity will carry the shot, and a bullet inherits your velocity so charging is brutal and fleeing is feeble. Solo Survival (escalating AI waves, five lives, instant play) plus live P2P deathmatch for 2–4, host-authoritative snapshot star, rematch inside the room with a running tally. Three modes that change the spatial problem not a dial: Skirmish (open arena), Nova (the star flares on a nine-second clock), Belt (weak star, dense rock). Spawns sit on a rotationally symmetric ring so no seat starts ahead — a fairness the mandatory balance sim measured rather than assumed (it found two real bugs and refuted two confident diagnoses; see src/modes.ts and tests/balance.test.ts). Finishes the 2026-07-13 slot the original run abandoned after 70 seconds.

games.name: gravity-golf
games.description: Slingshot physics mini-golf: pull back to launch and bend your ball around gravity wells (attractors pull, repulsors push, black holes swallow) into the hole across 9 procedural holes; solo, async seed-share, or live P2P race up to 6 players.

games.name: snake-royale
games.description: Classic snake in a shared arena: eat pellets to grow and score, avoid walls/snakes/your own tail; solo Endless score-attack plus live P2P Royale where the last snake alive wins.

games.name: rhythm-relay
games.description: Two-lane rhythm game: notes relay left↔right toward a hit line; tap in time to build a shared combo and score multiplier, misses drain energy; solo or take a lane each in live P2P co-op.

games.name: hexbloom
games.description: Turn-based hex-grid area control: pick a colour to recolour your territory and absorb every touching tile of it; largest territory when the board fills wins.

games.name: cipher-clash
games.description: Boggle-style word-grid race; drag touching letters to spell words, and in versus a word only scores for whoever claims it first.
